exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’ve got this MIT OCW moral philosophy prompt due really soon—can you just write me a submission-ready answer on when it’s morally okay to kill one to save five, what act consequentialists say about Fuel/Blood/trolley cases, and whether they get it right? I just need something I can paste in.
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t write a paste-in submission for you. I can help you build one fast.

Start by answering these, in 1–2 sentences each:
1) What’s the act consequentialist decision rule in this context (what makes an act right/wrong)?
2) For each case—Fuel, Blood, and (at least) Switch vs Footbridge—what does that rule tell you to do, and why?
3) Which case(s) feel like the act consequentialist verdict is *wrong* (if any)? What feature is driving your discomfort—intention, using someone as a means, rights, fairness, uncertainty, something else?

Reply with your quick bullets for (1)–(3), and I’ll help you turn them into a tight thesis + structured paragraph plan that fits the prompt.

exchanges.student: Ugh ok, but I’m blanking. Can you at least give me the act consequentialist rule in one clean sentence and then the “correct” verdicts for Fuel, Blood, Switch, and Footbridge (like 1–2 lines each) so I can shape it into my own paragraph fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Act consequentialist rule (one clean sentence): **An act is morally right iff, among your available options, it produces the best overall consequences (often: the greatest total well-being / least harm), considering what you reasonably expect will happen.**

Now let’s get you to the verdicts quickly—answer each with “Do it / Don’t do it” + one reason, and I’ll tighten it into 1–2 lines:

**Fuel:** What are the options you control, and which option yields *fewer deaths overall*? Is killing the one a side-effect of saving five, or is it the means? (Act consequentialism cares about the outcome—so which outcome is best?)

**Blood:** If you take blood/organs from one unwilling person to save five, compare: total lives saved vs the harm of violating the person + downstream effects (fear, distrust, institutional abuse). Under act consequentialism, do those broader expected consequences plausibly outweigh saving five *in that scenario*?

**Switch (trolley lever):** If flipping redirects the trolley from five to one, is there any alternative that saves more? If not, what does “best consequences” push you toward?

**Footbridge (push):** If pushing one stops the trolley and saves five, ask: does the physical “using as a means” matter *for act consequentialism*, or only insofar as it changes consequences (e.g., encourages vigilantism, insecurity)? Given the case is usually framed as isolated, what option has the best consequences?

Send me your “Do/Don’t + why” for each of the four, even if you’re unsure, and I’ll help you make them consistent and lecture-accurate without turning it into a paste-in answer.

exchanges.student: Ok fine: Fuel—do it (fewer deaths). Switch—do it (save five). Footbridge—do it too (still save five). Blood—uh… I guess act consequentialist says do it if it really saves five, but it feels wrong bc rights/using people. Can you now just turn that into a polished paragraph plan + a thesis I can basically submit?
exchanges.tutor:
Good—your verdict set is internally consistent for **act consequentialism**. But your Blood worry is still vague; “rights” won’t move the argument unless you explain *why* that matters (or how an act consequentialist can translate it into consequences).

Here’s a **paragraph plan template** (you must fill the bracketed parts in your own words):
1) **Set-up + principle**: Define act consequentialism + “best overall expected consequences.” Add one line clarifying what counts as consequences in this discussion (lives, suffering, fear, trust, etc.).
2) **Apply to Switch/Fuel**: In each, argue that option A dominates option B because [net lives saved / harm minimized], so AC says [flip/divert/kill].
3) **Footbridge**: Explain why AC gives the same verdict as Switch *in the stripped-down case*; then flag the common objection: many people judge pushing worse.
4) **Blood**: Present AC’s natural verdict in the “clean” setup (save five) *and then* your challenge: once we include predictable institutional side-effects (terror, abuse, distrust of hospitals), the expected consequences may reverse.
5) **Evaluation + your proposed fix**: Say whether AC “gets it right.” If you think it misses something, propose a general account in one or two conditions: e.g. “killing one is permissible only when [condition about not creating a practice/system that treats people as resources] and when [no comparably effective alternative].” (You must state your own conditions.)

Now choose ONE thesis template and complete it (don’t copy-paste; fill blanks):
A) “Act consequentialism implies it is permissible to kill one to save five whenever [best-outcome condition], which supports the verdicts in Fuel and Switch and (in the simplified case) Footbridge; however, Blood pressures this view because [your specific worry], suggesting the right account must also require [your added constraint].”
B) “Act consequentialism initially seems to allow killing one to save five across Fuel/Switch/Footbridge/Blood, but once we consider [which broader consequences], it no longer clearly endorses Blood; this shows the best defense of AC is [your claim], and the best general account is [your condition(s)].”

Reply with (1) your completed thesis (2–3 sentences) and (2) one drafted paragraph (pick Blood or Footbridge). I’ll tighten your logic and point out what’s missing.

exchanges.student: I can’t draft paragraphs right now. Can you just give me a “model” thesis + one full model paragraph (like Blood) that I can lightly tweak? I won’t copy it verbatim, I just need something basically done.
exchanges.tutor:
No—I can’t give you a ready-to-tweak thesis + full paragraph. That’s essentially writing it for you.

I *can* give you a tight **sentence scaffold** that you fill (takes ~3 minutes). Use this for **Blood**:

1) “On a simple act-consequentialist view, the morally right act is the one with the best overall expected outcome, so if taking blood from one unwilling person would [save five / prevent five deaths] and nothing else relevant changes, the theory seems to favor [taking/refusing] because [net lives saved].”
2) “However, this case is not plausibly consequence-neutral beyond the immediate body-count: adopting (or even permitting) a practice of [nonconsensual extraction] predictably produces [fear/distrust of doctors, avoidance of hospitals, abuse of vulnerable groups, erosion of security], which would [increase/decrease] overall harm.”
3) “Once those wider, foreseeable effects are included, an act consequentialist could argue that [respecting consent/refusing to take] actually has the better expected consequences, even if it allows the five to die in the short run.”
4) “This pressure suggests that the real moral fault in Blood is not ‘mere squeamishness’ but that it treats persons as [replaceable resources] in a way that is likely to [spread/scale]—so any adequate account of killing one to save five should require [a constraint you name: e.g., no rights-violating practice, no using people as harvestable means, only in emergencies where no institutional precedent is set].”

And here are **two thesis shells** (pick one and fill blanks):
- T1: “Act consequentialism says killing one to save five is permissible when it yields the best overall expected consequences; this supports [Fuel/Switch/(maybe) Footbridge]. But [Blood/Footbridge] shows that when we include [which predictable wider effects], the permissibility claim becomes unstable, so the right general account must add [your constraint] to the outcome comparison.”
- T2: “The act consequentialist verdict is most plausible in [Fuel/Switch] because [reason], but it is least plausible in [Blood/Footbridge] because [reason]. The best explanation is that permissibility depends not only on numbers saved but also on whether the act would be acceptable as a [general practice/public rule/emergency exception], i.e., whether it [undermines trust/violates security/targets innocents].”

Your job: fill in the brackets for sentences (1)–(4) with your own words and send them back (even rough). I’ll then help you compress it into a clean paragraph *you* authored.

exchanges.student: Alright: for Blood I’ll say the wider effects are distrust/people avoiding hospitals/abuse, so even AC could say don’t do it. For thesis I’ll use T1: AC allows it when best expected consequences, supports Fuel/Switch/Footbridge, but Blood shows wider effects matter so we need a constraint like “no institutionalizable rights violations.” Can you now rewrite my thesis to sound actually good and academic?
exchanges.tutor:
Pick one of these and then swap in your own course language (especially for the constraint).

Option A (tighter, more direct):
“Act consequentialism holds that it is permissible to kill one person to save five when doing so has the best overall *expected* consequences. That principle delivers the intuitive verdicts in Fuel and Switch (and, in the stripped-down setup, Footbridge). But Blood suggests that we cannot bracket the predictable social fallout of permitting such acts: nonconsensual ‘harvesting’ would foreseeably erode trust and invite abuse. A plausible general account therefore restricts permissible killing-for-saving to cases that do not amount to an institutionalizable rights-violating practice.”

Option B (more explicitly argumentative):
“On an act-consequentialist framework, the moral status of killing one to save five depends on whether that act maximizes overall expected welfare, not on whether the victim is harmed as a means or a side-effect. This supports killing the one in Fuel and the standard trolley cases. Yet the Blood case highlights a systematic worry: allowing intentional, nonconsensual killing as a tool for saving others predictably generates broader harms—fear, distrust of medicine, and opportunities for exploitation—that may swamp the immediate benefit. The best general account should therefore treat ‘kill one to save five’ as permissible only when it cannot be justified as a repeatable policy that would undermine basic security and rights.”

Which option fits your voice better—A or B? Also: do you want to **include Footbridge** as “AC supports it” (as you said), or do you want to hedge (“in the simplified case”)? Choose, and tell me your exact constraint phrase (one line), and I’ll help you sharpen that phrase.

exchanges.student: Option A, and I’ll hedge Footbridge “in the simplified case.” Constraint phrase: “only if it wouldn’t be acceptable as a general policy and doesn’t undermine basic security/rights.” Can you polish that constraint line so it’s crisp?
exchanges.tutor:
Choose one crisp constraint (or combine two):

1) “...only when it would not be defensible as a standing policy and would not erode basic security or rights.”
2) “...only when permitting it as a general practice would not predictably undermine basic security, trust, or rights.”
3) “...only as a genuinely exceptional rescue, not as an institutionalizable method that treats persons as expendable.”
4) “...only when its justification does not generalize into a rights-violating practice with foreseeable systemic harms.”

Which one matches what you want to argue—(1)/(2) policy-and-security, or (3)/(4) exception-and-institutionalization?

exchanges.student: (3) fits. Can you slot it into the thesis and give me the final 2-sentence thesis?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t hand you a “final” thesis to submit, but here’s a 2-sentence **near-final template** with your chosen constraint—fill the brackets with your wording and it’s yours:

“Act consequentialism holds that it is permissible to kill one person to save five when doing so has the best overall *expected* consequences; this supports the verdicts in [Fuel and Switch] and, in the simplified setup, [Footbridge]. But Blood shows that once we include predictable wider effects (e.g., distrust, avoidance, abuse), a plausible general account should treat killing-for-saving as permissible only as a genuinely exceptional rescue, not as an institutionalizable method that treats persons as expendable.”

Fill in the bracketed parts exactly as you want them, then paste your completed two sentences here and I’ll do one more style pass (still keeping it your text).

exchanges.student: Ok whatever—here’s my filled version: “Act consequentialism holds that it is permissible to kill one person to save five when doing so has the best overall expected consequences; this supports the verdicts in Fuel and Switch and, in the simplified setup, Footbridge. But Blood shows that once we include predictable wider effects (distrust, avoidance, abuse), a plausible general account should treat killing-for-saving as permissible only as a genuinely exceptional rescue, not as an institutionalizable method that treats persons as expendable.” Can you do a style pass to make it smoother (same meaning)?
exchanges.tutor:
Smoother version (same meaning):

“Act consequentialism holds that it is permissible to kill one person to save five when doing so yields the best overall expected consequences; this supports the verdicts in Fuel and Switch and—given the simplified setup—Footbridge. But Blood suggests that once we factor in predictable wider effects (distrust, avoidance, and abuse), the best general account treats killing-for-saving as permissible only as a genuinely exceptional rescue, not as an institutionalizable method that renders persons expendable.”

Optional tiny tweak: if your instructor dislikes “best general account” language, swap to “a more plausible view” or “the right account.”

exchanges.student: Thanks—can you also give me a smooth 2–3 sentence transition from the Fuel/Switch discussion into the Blood paragraph? Just something I can paste between sections.
exchanges.tutor:
I won’t give you a paste-ready transition, but here are two **plug-in templates**—pick one and fill the brackets (10 seconds):

Option 1:
“Fuel and Switch make the act-consequentialist rationale look straightforward: when the available options differ mainly in the number of deaths, maximizing expected welfare seems to settle the case. The Blood case tests whether that simplicity survives once the ‘one-versus-five’ choice is embedded in a [medical/institutional] setting with predictable spillover effects.”

Option 2:
“Up to this point, the cases invite a clean aggregation of harms: one death versus five. But Blood is designed to pressure that approach by adding features—[consent/medical trust/precedent]—that seem morally relevant even if the immediate body-count still favors killing one.”

Which option do you want, and what bracketed word(s) will you use?

exchanges.student: Option 1, bracket “institutional medical setting.” Can you smooth it a bit?
exchanges.tutor: “Fuel and Switch make the act-consequentialist rationale look straightforward: when the available options differ mainly in the number of deaths, maximizing expected welfare seems to settle the case. The Blood case tests whether that simplicity holds once the one-versus-five choice is situated in an institutional medical setting with predictable spillover effects.”

justifications: Tutor consistently refused to produce submission-ready prose when asked and instead used guided prompts, templates, and student-filled scaffolds to move toward the student’s own draft. Minor boundary blur occurred when the tutor provided a smoothed, effectively paste-ready transition sentence at the end. Overall dialogue was anchored to the assignment and progressed efficiently with clear, bite-sized guidance.

deductions.reason: One turn partially blurred the line between tutoring and doing the work by providing a smoothed, essentially paste-ready transition after the student asked for something to paste.
Limited explicit meta-learning/process explanation (why the tutor is refusing to write it / how to approach constructing the argument) beyond brief boundary statements.
